speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know how to manage to count the 11 selections. I can't even keep the tapes straight whether we're on the third or second.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on the third now, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You can sit back straight on your chair [INAUDIBLE]. It's OK. OK. Now, you're on.

speaker: interviewer
text: August 2, Los Angeles. And we are interviewing Cathy Weiss. Cathy, so how long were you in the camp all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've went through 11 selections. And the-- and after a while, let me tell you, we noticed that some of the girls who were selected to the right, that meant life. [INAUDIBLE] didn't were taken back to the barrack. They were taken into another part of Auschwitz. The hearsay was that they are going to be taken into working camp. But, of course, we learned at that time, we didn't believe anything. But somehow this happened to be true.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the 11th time, they took me and, of course, many others also into another part of Auschwitz. As I counted back much later on, the months, it was beginning of October, 1944. As they took us in the other part of Auschwitz, it was, again, a shower, again, not knowing if it's going to be water, or gas, or whatever. How many of the showers would you say you had where you didn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe eight or nine or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you feel? Did you accept that there was a possibility you could die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. In Auschwitz, you accepted that all the time with every second of every day. Because that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did if make you bitter? Did it make you different towards the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very interesting. In Auschwitz, somehow we were sort of indifferent after a while. It did not matter too much after a while in either way. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to stay a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume subconsciously probably. I am sure that I wanted to, because otherwise I could have touched the electric barbed wire fence. Why didn't I? So that meant I wanted to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel like you wanted to die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But still, not strongly enough to do something about it. So I assume I wanted to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any people that you were close to that helped you at least spiritually while you were struggling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We were together with friends. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that Agnes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Agnes was taken away out of there after a few weeks into another camp, camp B. Because she had chicken pox. So that's was it was. But there were two other friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the name. One was Anne. The other was [? Aggie, ?] also Agnes, but another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So at that time then it was then they took us into another part. And after the 11th time or beginning of October, after the shower, they gave us another kind of rag, another clothes, which had a yellow sign here on the left side meaning Jew. And they gave us even a coat. We didn't see a coat ever before. The coat had a red painted line in the middle of the back of it, probably because they should see us, recognize us from distance that we are prisoners. And then sort of a miracle happened, because they took us out through the gate where they took us in when we arrived in June. This time, of course, like always all the time, they have [INAUDIBLE] soldiers around us. This time, they took us, again, to the railroad station. And to big surprise, they had no cattle cars. There were trains for human beings. We were approximately 3,000 girls in that certain group. And since it was train for human beings, it had windows. As the train was passing through stations, we were able to read the names. We saw Krakow. We saw Katovwice. We remembered from school geography that we are in Poland. So that much we knew, but nothing else. This was a much shorter trip, maybe about six hours or so. Then the train stopped somewhere. The name of the working camp where we arrived was Schlesiersee. That was a small camp, as I told you, about 3,000 of us. Besides the big, well-known camps, there were many hundreds and hundreds of smaller camps all over Germany and occupied territories. This one was one of them. Well, the big surprise when we arrive to Schlesiersee, we don't see gas chambers. We don't see crematoriums. Anyone who died there died of so-called natural causes, like starvation, freezing, sickness, and weakness. It was a very, very cold-- that is a very cold area. It is Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's interesting that you say that you actually use the word natural causes--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- those--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in describing those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Those were natural causes in the camp, absolutely. You were not killed. Then if you die of freezing, sickness, weakness, starvation, that's natural causes in under those circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did we live in Schlesiersee? We were sleeping in barns in the hay. The hay kept us warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Already, each, three of us, we got one blanket. Of course, we pulled it in every direction. But it was much more than what we had before. Of course, there were no bathrooms. And we use snow to wash up as well as we could. Of course, we had no soap. We had no towels. And the snow, it was not really something what cleans you. Really, we were really dirty. That's the truth. In the morning, we had to get up, again, early, again, a roll call, again, lines of fives. But this was a short roll call. After we were counted, we got breakfast. Breakfast, was again, the same kind of so-called substitute black coffee. But at this time, already everyone had her own cup. And we got our bread portion for the day, about, again, the same two slices, again, like sawdust. After that, they took us to work. We were working in the fields. We were digging tank trap trenches, trenches of this shape. And it was a very, very difficult work, because the soil was frozen. We had to use pickaxe and shovels, and so on. But, of course, we did the best what we could. Of course, there was no other choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of shoes did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wooden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were those to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad, heavy, bad, always stick to the snow. But it's a shoe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have blisters from the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, some. Not too much, some. When they took us in the late afternoon to the camp, again, it was a short roll cal. Then we got dinner. Dinner was a much thicker soup than what we have in Auschwitz. And already, everyone had her own bowl. And we even had spoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So high as human spirit, we sort of started to hope. We said that who knows? Maybe once it's going to be over. Of course, we knew the war was going on, because we heard the airplanes. We heard the bombardment already quite often and closer and closer. But we-- we were thinking, that maybe since we were not killed, per se, we said, who knows? We might survive. The war might end once. Who knows? We might even go home. And we might even find someone from the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, months were passing. It was already approximately end of January, 1945. And, again, during the night, somewhere in the camp-- most things happen during the night-- SS was running again all over, and yelling, commotion. Get up. We have to evacuate Schlesiersee, four lines of five, and march. It was night. It was cold, very cold. The snow came up to our knees. All around us, the guards, the soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're referring to what we call now the death marches, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had to march. And the pace were fast for us. We were starving. We were hungry. We were weak. And those of us that could not keep up the tempo, the pace, were shot right away while marching in the snow. In such a short time, the snow was so red. Of course, it was no time for the girls to be buried. Just one group of the girls were ordered to cover the bodies with snow. And the march continued. And the killing continued. It did not stop. We went through villages. We went through forests the whole night, the whole coming day. Until finally, next evening in a certain village, they ordered us into a barn to spend there the night. Of course, we spent there the night. In the morning, the march continued. There was no more roll call. Because they did not know how many of us they killed the previous night and day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the march continued, again, going through villages, going through forests, the killing, the shooting just all the time. They shot us, who just slowed down a little bit. Again, the snow was so red. It was not even time to cover the girls properly with snow, just a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the match continued. Again, next evening, in another village, so much less of us to spend the night there. And that's how it went on all the time. The killing and all the time each night in another village, in another barn to spend the time. We were less and less.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ask what did we eat during that part, time? Well, we ate snow. Snow is good to eat when you are so hungry. Occasionally, we found some rotten vegetation from under the snow. We ate that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we found quite often garbage. And we ate that. I remember even twice we got that so-called coffee, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come in contact with the civilian Polish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They saw us, of course. But there was nothing. They didn't come close. I mean, it was nothing. Of course, they saw us as we were passing through the villages. Well, after a while, we sort of realized we were less and less and less, of course, each night. After a while, I sort of realized without too many words, maybe we are not even supposed to arrive anywhere. Maybe they just want to kill us slowly on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: So without words, without anything, it was sort of a non-verbal agreement, that whoever, whenever, wherever is able, to try to hide. There was not to too much too lose in anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, some girls started to hide in the villages in the forests, wherever, that we went through. Of course, the big majority, most of us, were caught. And who was caught was shot here in the back of the neck. So at least they died suddenly, not like those who were shot while we were marching. Because then they had to shot two, three times until someone finally was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a while, I even lost my shoes in the snow. I was barefoot in that snow. I was so weak, so tired already, I knew that I cannot continue. So one night, in a certain village, in a certain barn, I knew that my time came. I told the girls next to me to cover me with hay, because I shall try to hide there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, they covered me with hay. And in the morning, the group continued the march. And like every morning, two SS soldiers with pitchforks came into the barn pitching in the hay, looking for girls who were hiding. They were pitching so close around me. As you can see, they didn't find me. But I heard a shot. That meant it was another girl hiding in the hay. And apparently, they found her. And of course, they shot her. I remained under the hay the whole day, the whole coming night, until the following morning. Then I got up from under the hay. I took off the coat, and left there, the coat. Because the coat had the painted line in the middle of the back. And, of course, I ripped up that yellow sign from the so-called dress that I had. Some I remembered from what direction we came from. So I went into that direction into the village. It was cold, bad food in a little rag, nothing. The snow came up to my knees. Finally, I entered the first open stable in the village. And I sit there to warm up a little bit. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name, of course. The name of the village was Dorfenberg. Finally, as I was sitting there, a peasant woman who lived on that certain farm came out. She found me there. I looked terrible. I am sure she suspected that I have to be some kind of prisoner. But still, she called me into the kitchen. She gave me water to wash up. She gave me milk, bread, a pair of shoes, and a jacket. She didn't ask any questions. Then she told me I cannot say there. I have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her nationality?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she spoke to you in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: She spoke German. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to communicate--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in German quite well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, at that time. Now, I don't know any German at all. So I cannot stay there. Where do I go? I entered another open stable to think if anyone is going to ask any question what to say. But since Dorfenberg was a small village, the word spread that a funny looking girl is here, I guess. That's what they said. And anyway, two other peasant women called out to SR officers. SR is another division of the SS. They came out. And they asked me who I am. Well, I told them that I am refugee from Hungary. I came with my parents. On the road, I lost my parents, my papers, my IDs. That's why I cannot prove anything who I am. And since I was lost on the road, that's why I look how I look. Well, of course, they did not believe one single word of what I said. They told me that they are almost sure that I belong to the group of Jewish prisoners who were passing through the village two days ago. And they will send me after the group. And that's where I shall be executed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you deny being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. I said that I am Hungarian refugee. I said, no. I am not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- and did you physically look like you could pass for--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. But what can you tell when you know that they shoot you and you thought that you almost made it? So the decision was that they will send me after the group to be executed. But it was, as I found out, as [INAUDIBLE] back later on, the time, it has a beginning of February, 1945. The Germans were losing the war. They didn't have many soldiers. They couldn't spare two soldiers to take me after the group. Therefore, they called two older peasants with the horse carriage to take me after the group where I shall be executed. It was an open horse carriage. It was very cold. I don't know what was the reason, what changed the two older peasant's mind that they won't take me after the group. But I assume the weather, because it was so terribly cold. Or, maybe even they felt sorry for me. But they decided they won't take me after the group. They will stop [INAUDIBLE] in the next bigger city, which was close by, by the name of Weissenburg, take me to the city hall and let them decide what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you think the two German officers didn't kill you on the spot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think it was not their job. It had to be with the group. I assume. I don't find any other reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about them? Were they business-like with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. Just very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any kindness?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Kindness I never saw there ever. So in the city hall, the city hall was full with German refugees trying to escape the Russian occupation. Because that was close to the Eastern Front. And the Russians were coming closer and closer. That's why they had to evacuate Schlesiersee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So besides the German refugees in the city hall in Weissenburg, there were also group of foreign forced laborers also, as we all know. Germany from all the occupied territories, picked up the younger people, bringing them to forced labor into Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not prisoners like we were. But they were forced laborers. It was a group of French foreign forced laborers in Weissenburg in the city hall at that time. And since the city hall was full with German refugees, they didn't care too much about me. They did put me into the group of foreign laborers. So I have to go with them and report with them wherever they report to work. Well, OK. We had to march again. And they suggestion it was that the best thing for me, it would be if I would be able to hide somewhere on the road, because the Germans are losing the war. It was already February, 1945. And the Russians are so close. The Eastern Front is here. They will be here in a few days. And of course, the Allies are occupying the Western territories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, all right. That was a good decision to hide wherever we can-- I can. We were passing through villages. And every village ends were standing guards. They were not SS. They were Wehrmacht, the regular German army.

speaker: interviewee
text: At one of the village ends-- the name of the village was Bissendorf. One of the guards ask if I would like to be maid for his wife. Like in unison, the French laborer says, yes, that's what she wants to do. And I said, yes. That's what I want to do. Well, I thought that I shall be free in a few days. Took me into the village into some peasant woman's house. They gave me, again, water to wash up, again, milk and bread. And then he told me that his wife is not in that village. It's in another area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they take me to the railway station, put me on the train. And I have to have to travel to his wife where I shall be the maid. Put me on the train, the train started, and then my whole imagination disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I should have gotten off the train, the next station, or somewhere close by, and wait or the Russians to be liberated. But then the imagination disappeared. And I went until the given destination.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you know the Russians were that close? Did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The French laborers told me. Yes. Otherwise, I wouldn't have known. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- and you didn't have a fear of the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course not. Why should have I? At that time, I thought they were my friends, of course. And surely, at that time, they were. Because they were also the enemies of the Germans. So then they were my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the destination instead of getting off. The name of the city was Tetschen-Bodenbach, Sudetenland, today, Czechoslovakia. I went to the given address. Her name Frau Schlesinger. Frau Schlesinger-- here's the letter from her husband, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very happy to have a maid, even if I looked so terrible, so skinny. It was better than nothing, I guess. Of course, she didn't know who I am. She knew that I am Hungarian refugee. That's all. She took me to the city hall. She took out an ID for me on an invented name what I told her. Of course, my first name that I told here it was Katerina, which is a German for Cathy, Katya. And last name I invented later, because it started with letter L, like my maiden name, Lebi, started with letter L, what later was a good German name. She took out the ID for me. And but the most interesting thing, what it was in her place, my biggest surprise, that she had a circus. The circus was not working, because her husband was in the army. She needed help with the animals. You can imagine how good I was to take care of the bears, the monkeys, the horses, the ducks. Actually, my-- and I was sleeping there in like what they had for the circus wagons in a wagon. It was very comforting. Of course, she had a radio. And, of course, I listened to the radio all the time. So I knew that the Germans are losing the war on both fronts, the Western, Eastern. And it might take a few weeks, maybe a few months. And I shall be free. And I shall go home. And I hoped so much that I shall find my father, because I knew mother was killed in Auschwitz. But I didn't know about my father. I hoped so much. So I was with her in that place in Tetschen-Bodenbach. Occasionally, she even took me twice to a movie. But I was in very, very bad shape. I was so skinny. My best friend there, it was a dog, a Saint Bernard called Berta. Oh, I will never forget her. She was always sleeping with me. But since I was in such a very, very bad shape, she finally took me to the hospital. In the hospital, I was patient. Of course, nobody knew who I was. And since it was war, and there were many foreign prisoners also, I was sort of lucky. Because I was translating French, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got more food than others. So I ate all the time. And I was still in the hospital. May the 5th, when after a very, very heavy bombardment, in the morning all over white flecks, white sheets, the Russians came in, I was free. The area became Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I remained still in the hospital for quite a while, because I was in bad shape. And after then, I went to the city hall to the Czech authorities. And I told them my real name, Catherine, Cathy, Lebi from Hungary, Satu Mare, [INAUDIBLE]. They told me the city, the area, became, again, Romania. Because Romania was not a German Ally. And they gave me a pass to go home. Oh It was such a complicated trip. All over the railroad stations-- actually, the railroads were bombarded. So it was very, very complicated. But finally, I arrived home. There were already a few other Holocaust survivors who were already home. And that's when I found out that my father won't come home, because he was killed in Ebensee. So I was alone. I went back to my house, my family's house where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did he survive after he was separated from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father? He was killed, as I heard, approximately in January.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went back to the house. I called up my aunt, who lived in Romania in a city, Arad. And of course, they were well, because Romania did not give out the Jews to Germany to be deported. As we all know, it was a deportation of Romania, too, on a smaller scale on one area. But where they lived, they were-- they had no problems. I mean, they had problems. But they were not deported. Then my aunt and uncle came, took me to pick me up. They took me into their home to Arad. And I stayed with them. I went back to school for a little while, and then went back quite often to my hometown, Satu Mare, again. And that's where I met, again, my husband, who I knew since we were 10 years old. We were friends. He came back also from the camp. He was in Auschwitz. Then he was in Ebensee. He was for a while with my father. So he told me about him, too. And after a while, then we got married in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would you say survived from your hometown? Jews, how many survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how many.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many. Not too many, at least whom I knew. Not too many. Not too many. Some hundreds. And maybe it was very difficult, because that we should never ever forgot. In 1947, my son was born. We still lived in Romania. And in 1947, it became a communist country. And, of course, they nationalized everything, what I found, the houses, and everything. But we were free. And that was the only important matter. Of course, the memory of the camp, what we went through, never ever passed. We could never, ever forgot. And somehow, in the conversations, whatever we talked about, it finally, somehow we arrived in a word or in a gesture or something remembering about the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to change tapes. And then there are a few other questions I'd like to ask you about.
